1: Elkanah's five-generation Ephraimite genealogy establishes Samuel's origins in the tribal structure of Israel. The genealogy is transitional setup: Samuel will be the last judge and first of the great prophets, the human hinge between the age of judges and the age of kings. Christ's genealogy in Matthew 1 serves the same structural function — establishing his place in the covenant history before announcing the decisive new beginning. The genealogical form signals that what follows is the continuation and climax of YHWH's covenant story.
2: Hannah's barrenness (YHWH had closed her womb, v5) places her in the line of the barren women through whom YHWH sovereignly initiates his redemptive purposes: Sarah (Genesis 11:30; 1 Samuel 21:1-2), Rebekah (Genesis 25:21), Rachel (Genesis 29:31; 1 Samuel 30:22-24), and Samson's unnamed mother (Judges 13:2-3). Each barren woman who conceives by divine opening is a type of the impossible birth that YHWH alone can accomplish. The pattern culminates in Mary's virginal conception — the ultimate impossible birth, where the womb is opened not from barrenness but from virginity, and the child born is not a prophet or judge but the Son of God himself.
3: Elkanah's annual pilgrimage to Shiloh to worship and sacrifice before YHWH Sabaoth establishes the household's covenant fidelity. The faithful father making annual pilgrimage to the sanctuary echoes the pattern of Joseph and Mary's annual pilgrimage to Jerusalem (Luke 2:41-42) — the same act of covenant devotion that frames the episode of twelve-year-old Jesus remaining in the temple. In both cases, the annual pilgrimage to the sanctuary becomes the occasion for a divine disclosure about the child who will change history.
4: Elkanah distributes portions to Peninnah and her children at the festival sacrifice — the ordinary economics of a polygamous household navigating covenant worship. The distribution of sacrificial portions at the annual feast is the backdrop against which Hannah's double portion and empty womb create the narrative tension. Christ at the Last Supper distributes the elements — his body and blood — to his disciples: the sacrificial meal becomes the defining act of the new covenant as it was the context for the Shiloh covenant meal.
5: Elkanah gives Hannah a double portion because he loves her, though YHWH had closed her womb. The double portion (manah ʾaḥat ʾappāyim) of special love for the childless wife anticipates the pattern of divine favor on the humanly disadvantaged. YHWH's closing of the womb is sovereign, purposeful, and temporary — a preparation for intervention, not a permanent exclusion. The pattern: special love accompanies the unfulfilled promise, and YHWH's own timing will supply what human biology cannot. This is the economy of grace that Paul identifies in 1 Corinthians 1:27-29: YHWH chooses what is humanly unable to produce.
6: Peninnah provokes Hannah bitterly year after year because YHWH had closed her womb — the adversary mocking the one whom YHWH has chosen. This structure of the chosen-but-disadvantaged person mocked by a rival echoes throughout the OT: Joseph mocked by brothers, David despised by Saul, the Servant of Isaiah despised and rejected. The adversary's provocation intensifies the distress that drives the chosen one to prayer. Christ, despised and rejected by men (Isaiah 53:3), is the ultimate fulfillment of this pattern: the Chosen One of God most deeply mocked at Calvary.
7: The annual provocation — every time Hannah went up to the house of YHWH — makes the sanctuary itself the site of her suffering. The place of YHWH's presence is the place where Hannah's unresolved pain is most acute. This is the theology of lament: the pain is not hidden from YHWH but brought to his face. Christ in Gethsemane brings his deepest anguish to the Father in prayer (Luke 22:41-44) — the same pattern of the beloved of God not suppressing suffering but expressing it in the Father's presence.
8: Elkanah's comfort — “Am I not more to you than ten sons?” — is genuine but insufficient. The human love of the closest earthly relationship cannot substitute for the unfulfilled divine promise. Hannah's grief persists because her longing is ultimately about YHWH's purpose, not merely personal desire. Christ's disciples are comforted by his presence but still await the full fulfillment of the kingdom's coming; human relationship with Christ is genuine but not yet the complete satisfaction it will be in the resurrection (1 John 3:2: we shall be like him when we see him face to face).
9: Hannah rises after the meal and goes to pray before YHWH while Eli sits at the doorpost of the temple. The spatial arrangement — Hannah approaching the sanctuary while the priest sits at the threshold — is significant. The legitimate sacrificial system, represented by Eli, is present but passive; the direct, personal, desperate prayer of a covenant woman is the active channel for the divine response. Christ's high priestly ministry similarly positions him as the one who, from the true sanctuary, is responsive to the desperate prayers of his people (Hebrews 7:25: he always lives to make intercession for those who draw near to God through him).
10: Hannah prayed to YHWH with deep bitterness of soul, weeping bitterly (tibke bāḵôʾ). The intensity of her prayer — the bitterness, the tears, the completeness of her desperation — is the pattern of authentic petition that characterizes OT lament and NT prayer. Jesus wept (John 11:35) at Lazarus's tomb; he offered up prayers with loud cries and tears during his earthly life (Hebrews 5:7). The Servant who carries sorrows (Isaiah 53:4) is pre-figured in the sorrowful woman whose prayer becomes the instrument of divine action.
11: Hannah's vow: if YHWH will look on her misery and give her a son, she will give him back to YHWH all his days, and no razor shall touch his head — a Nazirite dedication. The vow structure is reciprocal: Hannah gives YHWH her need, and in return gives back whatever is given. This is the theology of consecration: the gift received from YHWH is returned to YHWH for his purposes. Christ “for the joy set before him endured the cross” (Hebrews 12:2) — wholly consecrated to the Father's purposes. Hannah's Nazirite vow for Samuel (never cutting the hair) signals that this son belongs entirely to YHWH's service from birth.
12: As Hannah continued praying before YHWH, Eli watched her mouth. The priest's surveillance of the praying woman — assuming drunkenness rather than genuine devotion — is a first marker of Eli's diminished spiritual perception. He sees the form of prayer (moving lips) but cannot discern its substance (deep intercession). The religious establishment observing but not understanding the authentic spiritual transaction taking place is a pattern Christ addresses repeatedly: the Pharisees who see his miracles but attribute them to Beelzebul (Matthew 12:24), the scribes who observe his ministry but cannot perceive its true nature.
13: Hannah prays in her heart — her lips move but her voice is not heard. Eli, seeing only the external behavior, judges her as drunk. The misidentification of genuine prayer as intoxication is the ironic contrast between surface reading and spiritual reality. Paul describes the Spirit's deep intercession in similar terms: “the Spirit himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words” (Romans 8:26) — prayer that cannot be verbalized is the most profound form. Hannah's silent but deep prayer is the model that Paul will theologically articulate for those whose Spirit-filled intercession goes beyond ordinary language.
14: Eli's rebuke — “How long are you going to be drunk? Put your wine away” — is an institutional false accusation of the genuinely righteous. The pattern of the legitimate religious authority wrongly accusing the genuinely faithful is the structure fulfilled in the trial of Jesus: the high priest and council pronouncing Jesus guilty on the basis of misreading who he truly is (Matthew 26:65-66: “he has spoken blasphemy”). In both cases the institutional representative of the covenant community misidentifies the most faithful person before him.
15: Hannah's defense — “I am a woman deeply troubled in spirit; I have drunk neither wine nor strong drink, but I have been pouring out my soul before YHWH” — is an appeal to inner spiritual reality over external appearance. The phrase “pouring out my soul” (špk napšî) is the idiom for total self-disclosure before YHWH (Psalms 62:8; Lamentations 2:19). Christ's high priestly prayer in John 17 is the supreme example of this complete outpouring before the Father on behalf of his people. Hannah models the transparent, vulnerable prayer that characterizes the covenant person in extremity.
16: “Do not take your servant for a worthless woman (bat-bĕliyyaʿal) — for I have been speaking from my great anxiety and grief.” The self-designation as ʾāmāh (female servant/slave) twice in this verse is Hannah's identification with the lowest covenant category — not claiming rights but appealing entirely to YHWH's grace. This is the posture of the publican in Jesus's parable (Luke 18:13: “God, be merciful to me, a sinner”) versus the Pharisee's self-presentation. Christ himself takes the form of a servant (doulos, Philippians 2:7) — the divine Son occupying the lowest relational position to accomplish what no other position could accomplish.
17: Eli's pronouncement — “Go in peace, and may the God of Israel grant your petition that you have asked of him” — functions as a priestly oracle. The priest who moments ago accused Hannah of drunkenness now speaks YHWH's word of blessing over her. The same human authority that wrongly accused becomes the instrument of divine commissioning. This is the pattern of Acts 2: the same Jerusalem crowd that participated in crucifying Jesus becomes the first recipients of Peter's Pentecost sermon and the first converts. The instrument of condemnation becomes the instrument of blessing.
18: “Let your servant find favor in your eyes” — and she ate, drank, and her face was sad no more. The transformation is immediate: before any physical sign of answer, before conception, Hannah's face changes. This is faith receiving the promise as though it were already accomplished — what Hebrews 11:1 defines as “the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.” Christ's pattern of declaring “your faith has saved/healed you” (Luke 7:50; 1 Samuel 17:19) operates on the same principle: the divine word received in faith effects the transformation before the physical evidence is complete.
19: Elkanah knew his wife, and YHWH remembered her (wayyizkorāh YHWH). The divine “remembering” is the covenant-activation verb: YHWH remembers Noah (Genesis 8:1), remembers Abraham when destroying Sodom (Genesis 19:29), remembers Rachel (Genesis 30:22). Divine remembering is not recovery from forgetfulness but the moment of covenant promise being enacted. The same verb appears in Luke 1:54-55 in Mary's Magnificat: “he has helped his servant Israel, in remembrance of his mercy, as he spoke to our fathers” — the Magnificat explicitly identifies Christ's coming as YHWH's covenant remembering.
20: Hannah conceived and bore a son and called his name Samuel — “for I have asked him of YHWH.” The name šĕmûʾēl (asked/heard of God) encodes the theology of petitionary prayer answered: the child's entire existence is a declaration that YHWH hears prayer. Contrast Jesus: his name is “YHWH saves” (yēšûaʿ) — the name encodes not the prayer but the answer; not the asking but the salvation that is the ultimate answer to all human asking (Matthew 1:21: “you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins”). Samuel is the asked-for; Jesus is the saving answer.
21: Elkanah goes to offer the yearly sacrifice and fulfill his vow. Hannah stays back, explaining she will bring the child when weaned — “that he may appear before YHWH and dwell there forever.” The phrase “dwell there forever” (yāšaḇ šām ʿaḏ-ʿôlām) anticipates the permanent dedication of Samuel to Shiloh. Christ, who ascended and is seated permanently at the right hand of the Father (Hebrews 1:3; 1 Samuel 10:12), “dwells there forever” in the truest sense — his high priestly presence in the heavenly sanctuary is permanent, not seasonal like the Levitical round of annual sacrifices.
22: Hannah will not go up until the child is weaned — “then I will bring him, that he may appear before YHWH and remain there forever.” The weaning period (typically 2-3 years in the ancient world) was the period of formation under the mother before formal dedication. Christ's “hidden years” in Nazareth — the 30 years before his public ministry — serve a parallel function: the period of formation (growing in wisdom and stature, Luke 2:52) before appearing publicly as YHWH's servant. Both Samuel and Christ are prepared in hiddenness before their public dedication.
23: Elkanah's blessing — “Only, may YHWH confirm his word” (yāqēm YHWH ʾet-dĕḇārô) — is the faith-confession of the father who entrusts the divine promise to YHWH's faithfulness. The petition that YHWH establish/confirm his word is the prayer of every generation awaiting messianic fulfillment. Simeon in Luke 2:25-32 represents the accumulated waiting of all who prayed “may YHWH confirm his word” — and at the temple presentation his prayer is answered: he holds the one who is YHWH's word confirmed.
24: Hannah brings the weaned Samuel to Shiloh with a three-year-old bull, an ephah of flour, and a skin of wine — a substantial offering accompanying the dedication. The parallel in Luke 2:22-24 is explicit: Mary and Joseph bring the infant Jesus to the temple in Jerusalem to present him to the Lord, as it is written in the law of Moses. The dedication of the firstborn son at the sanctuary — Samuel at Shiloh, Jesus at Jerusalem — is the same typological structure. Both presentations are accompanied by sacrificial offerings; both children are formally consecrated to YHWH's service at the sanctuary.
25: The bull was slaughtered and the child brought to Eli. The sacrificial killing that accompanies Samuel's presentation at the sanctuary is the ritual backdrop that prefigures the greater presentation: Christ’s presentation before the Father is accomplished through his own sacrificial death, not an animal substitute. The book of Hebrews (Hebrews 9:11-14; 1 Samuel 10:5-10) makes this explicit: the animal sacrifices of the Levitical system were shadows of the one true self-offering that would abolish the need for further sacrifice. Samuel is presented at the sanctuary after the bull's death; Christ enters the true sanctuary through his own blood (Hebrews 9:12).
26: Hannah’s self-identification: “I am the woman who was standing here in your presence praying to YHWH.” The fulfilled-prayer testimony — “for this child I prayed, and YHWH has granted me my petition” — is the pattern of all realized-promise confession. The NT counterpart is Simeon's “Lord, now you are letting your servant depart in peace, according to your word; for my eyes have seen your salvation” (Luke 2:29-30) — the long-prayed-for becomes the held-in-arms reality. Hannah models the testimony that transforms private faith into public witness.
27: “For this child I prayed, and YHWH has granted me my petition that I asked of him.” The completion-of-prayer testimony is the basis for the return-to-YHWH dedication: because YHWH answered, the gift is returned. The pattern of received grace motivating consecration is Paul’s logic in Romans 12:1: “in view of God’s mercies, offer your bodies as a living sacrifice.” Christ himself offers the supreme example: receiving everything from the Father (John 3:35: all things have been given into his hands), he returns everything to the Father in the cross and exaltation (1 Corinthians 15:28: the Son himself will be subjected to him).
28: “Therefore I have given him back to YHWH. As long as he lives, he belongs to YHWH.” Samuel’s lifelong dedication to YHWH at Shiloh is the pattern for the consecrated life that receives its purpose entirely from YHWH’s call. Christ’s self-description “I do not seek my own will but the will of him who sent me” (John 5:30) is the perfect instantiation of this dedication: completely given to YHWH’s purpose, belonging entirely to the Father. The hinnēnî (here I am) formula — which Samuel will use when YHWH calls him in 1 Samuel 3 — is the verbal expression of this total consecration.
